*** Add File: d:\__MY APPS\ad free podcast player\apps\server\windows\README.md
+# Windows Local Stack
+
+This folder holds the local Windows setup for the server stack when you want to run the API and web UI on the same machine.
+
+## What It Does
+
+- Keeps the remote Linux deployment unchanged
+- Runs the PHP API locally under WAMP or another Apache + PHP stack
+- Hosts the built web app locally in the same Apache site
+- Lets you disable the local UI without shutting down the API
+
+## Suggested Layout
+
+- `C:\wamp64\www\adfree-api` for the PHP API entrypoint
+- `C:\wamp64\www\adfree-web` for the built web app
+- `C:\wamp64\storage` for SQLite, queue files, uploads, output, and artifacts
+
+## UI Disable Toggle
+
+Create this file to disable the local UI:
+
+`C:\wamp64\www\adfree-web\.ui-disabled`
+
+Remove that file to turn the UI back on.
+
+The Apache rules in `apps/web/public/.htaccess` block the whole local UI directory when that file exists, but the API stays live.
+
+## Apache VHost
+
+Use `apps/server/windows/wamp-vhost.example.conf` as the starting point for your WAMP virtual host.
+
+It sets the storage environment variables expected by `apps/server/api/public/index.php` and keeps the API and UI paths separate.
+
+## Sync Script
+
+Use `apps/server/windows/setup-local.ps1` to copy the built web assets and API entrypoint into your WAMP site root.
+
+The script accepts a `-DisableUi` switch so you can leave the API exposed while turning the UI off.
*** Add File: d:\__MY APPS\ad free podcast player\apps\server\windows\wamp-vhost.example.conf
+<VirtualHost *:80>
+    ServerName adfree.local
+    DocumentRoot "C:/wamp64/www"
+
+    <Directory "C:/wamp64/www">
+        AllowOverride All
+        Require all granted
+    </Directory>
+
+    Alias /adfree-api "C:/wamp64/www/adfree-api"
+    Alias /adfree-web "C:/wamp64/www/adfree-web"
+
+    <Directory "C:/wamp64/www/adfree-api">
+        AllowOverride None
+        Require all granted
+    </Directory>
+
+    <Directory "C:/wamp64/www/adfree-web">
+        AllowOverride All
+        Require all granted
+    </Directory>
+
+    SetEnv APP_STORAGE "C:/wamp64/storage"
+    SetEnv DB_PATH "C:/wamp64/storage/jobs.sqlite"
+    SetEnv QUEUE_DIR "C:/wamp64/storage/queue"
+    SetEnv INPUT_DIR "C:/wamp64/storage/input"
+    SetEnv OUTPUT_DIR "C:/wamp64/storage/output"
+    SetEnv ARTIFACTS_DIR "C:/wamp64/storage/artifacts"
+    SetEnv WORKER_HEARTBEAT "C:/wamp64/storage/worker-heartbeat.json"
+    SetEnv WORKER_LOCK_DIR "C:/wamp64/storage/worker.lock"
+    SetEnv APP_BASE_PATH ""
+</VirtualHost>
*** Add File: d:\__MY APPS\ad free podcast player\apps\server\windows\setup-local.ps1
+param(
+    [string]$RepoRoot = (Resolve-Path (Join-Path $PSScriptRoot '..\..\..')).Path,
+    [string]$WampRoot = 'C:\wamp64\www',
+    [switch]$DisableUi
+)
+
+$webDist = Join-Path $RepoRoot 'apps\web\dist'
+$apiPublic = Join-Path $RepoRoot 'apps\server\api\public'
+$webTarget = Join-Path $WampRoot 'adfree-web'
+$apiTarget = Join-Path $WampRoot 'adfree-api'
+$storageRoot = 'C:\wamp64\storage'
+
+if (-not (Test-Path $webDist)) {
+    throw "Missing web build output at $webDist. Run the web build first."
+}
+
+New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $webTarget, $apiTarget, $storageRoot | Out-Null
+
+Copy-Item (Join-Path $webDist '*') $webTarget -Recurse -Force
+Copy-Item (Join-Path $apiPublic 'index.php') (Join-Path $apiTarget 'index.php') -Force
+
+if ($DisableUi) {
+    New-Item -ItemType File -Force -Path (Join-Path $webTarget '.ui-disabled') | Out-Null
+} else {
+    Remove-Item -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue (Join-Path $webTarget '.ui-disabled')
+}
+
+Write-Host "Synced web UI to $webTarget"
+Write-Host "Synced API to $apiTarget"
+Write-Host "Storage root expected at $storageRoot"
+Write-Host "UI enabled: $(-not $DisableUi)"
*** End Patch"}

arguments.content:
# Windows Local Stack

This folder holds the local Windows setup for the server stack when you want to run the API and web UI on the same machine.

## What It Does

- Keeps the remote Linux deployment unchanged
- Runs the PHP API locally under WAMP or another Apache + PHP stack
- Hosts the built web app locally in the same Apache site
- Lets you disable the local UI without shutting down the API

## Suggested Layout

- `C:\wamp64\www\adfree-api` for the PHP API entrypoint
- `C:\wamp64\www\adfree-web` for the built web app
- `C:\wamp64\storage` for SQLite, queue files, uploads, output, and artifacts

## UI Disable Toggle

Create this file to disable the local UI:

`C:\wamp64\www\adfree-web\.ui-disabled`

Remove that file to turn the UI back on.

The Apache rules in `apps/web/public/.htaccess` block the whole local UI directory when that file exists, but the API stays live.

## Apache VHost

Use `apps/server/windows/wamp-vhost.example.conf` as the starting point for your WAMP virtual host.

It sets the storage environment variables expected by `apps/server/api/public/index.php` and keeps the API and UI paths separate.

## Sync Script

Use `apps/server/windows/setup-local.ps1` to copy the built web assets and API entrypoint into your WAMP site root.

The script accepts a `-DisableUi` switch so you can leave the API exposed while turning the UI off.

param(
    [string]$RepoRoot = (Resolve-Path (Join-Path $PSScriptRoot '..\..\..')).Path,
    [string]$WampRoot = 'C:\wamp64\www',
    [switch]$DisableUi
)

$webDist = Join-Path $RepoRoot 'apps\web\dist'
$apiPublic = Join-Path $RepoRoot 'apps\server\api\public'
$webTarget = Join-Path $WampRoot 'adfree-web'
$apiTarget = Join-Path $WampRoot 'adfree-api'
$storageRoot = 'C:\wamp64\storage'

if (-not (Test-Path $webDist)) {
    throw "Missing web build output at $webDist. Run the web build first."
}

New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $webTarget, $apiTarget, $storageRoot | Out-Null

Copy-Item (Join-Path $webDist '*') $webTarget -Recurse -Force
Copy-Item (Join-Path $apiPublic 'index.php') (Join-Path $apiTarget 'index.php') -Force

if ($DisableUi) {
    New-Item -ItemType File -Force -Path (Join-Path $webTarget '.ui-disabled') | Out-Null
} else {
    Remove-Item -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue (Join-Path $webTarget '.ui-disabled')
}

Write-Host "Synced web UI to $webTarget"
Write-Host "Synced API to $apiTarget"
Write-Host "Storage root expected at $storageRoot"
Write-Host "UI enabled: $(-not $DisableUi)"

data.content: how will I access this from the web now?

WAMP alone is only reachable on your local network. To reach it from the web (and from Android on a mobile network), you need a tunnel that punches out from your machine to a public URL — without opening router ports.

**Best option: Cloudflare Tunnel (free)**

1. Install `cloudflared` on this machine
2. Authenticate it to a free Cloudflare account
3. Point it at `http://localhost:80` (where WAMP listens)
4. Cloudflare gives you a stable public URL like `https://your-name.trycloudflare.com` or a custom subdomain on a domain you own

Traffic goes: internet → Cloudflare's edge → encrypted tunnel → your machine → WAMP. Your machine never has an open inbound port.

**Restricting access** (so random people can't hit the API):

The cleanest option that fits what you already have is an API token check in the PHP API. I can add a single `APP_API_TOKEN` env var to the API — if it's set, every request must include `Authorization: Bearer <token>`. data.content: is there a way to do this without cloudflare or third party service?

Yes. You already have everything you need.

**Option 1: SSH reverse tunnel through your existing server (best fit)**

You already pay for and control the remote Linux server. You can punch a tunnel from your Windows machine to it using SSH — no new services involved.

How it works:
1. On your Windows machine, run: `ssh -R 8081:localhost:80 user@your-server`
2. That makes port `8081` on your remote server forward to port `80` on your local WAMP
3. You add a reverse proxy rule on your remote server's Apache so `/local-adfree-api/` proxies to `localhost:8081`
4. The web and Android clients point at `https://adsbegone.sitesindevelopment.com/local-adfree-api/`

Traffic path: internet → your remote server → SSH tunnel → your Windows machine → WAMP.

Upsides: uses infrastructure you already own, no new accounts, encrypted end-to-end.  
Downsides: the tunnel must stay connected while you want it live (you'd run it as a Windows scheduled task or background process), and traffic routes through the remote server adding a little latency.

---

**Option 2: Direct port forwarding on your router**

Open port 80 on your router and forward it to your Windows machine's local IP. Your public IP becomes the address.

Upsides: no middleman at all, lowest latency.  
Downsides: your ISP may block port 80 (very common), your public IP changes unless you set up DDNS, and your machine has a direct inbound port open to the internet.

---

**Recommendation:** Option 1 with the SSH tunnel. It uses your existing server and keeps the same security posture you already have. Want me to wire it up — add the reverse proxy config for the remote server and a Windows startup script to keep the tunnel alive?

data.content: I don't get it.  how can my remote server access my local server if I don't open a port?

The key is **direction**. You never need to accept an inbound connection — your machine always initiates outward.

Here's what actually happens:

```
Your Windows machine  ──outbound SSH──▶  Remote server
                       (you initiated it,
                        no inbound port needed)
```

Once that outbound SSH connection exists, both ends can use it in either direction — like a two-way pipe. The remote server can push traffic back through the pipe your machine already opened.

Compare to the alternative:

```
Internet user  ──inbound──▶  Your Windows machine
                              (needs an open port,
                               this is what you'd want to avoid)
```

A practical analogy: it's like you calling someone on the phone. Once the call is connected, they can talk back to you — even though they didn't call you and don't have your number. You made the outbound call; the line is open in both directions.

So the sequence is:

1. Your Windows machine runs `ssh -R ...` — one outbound connection to your server
2. A web request arrives at your remote server
3. The server pushes that request back through the already-open SSH pipe to your machine
4. Your machine handles it with WAMP and sends the response back the same way

No inbound port on your Windows machine. Your router never has to be touched.

data.content: is this related to wamp, or independent then?  will this be persistant or do I need to do something each time I start my windows computer?

**Independent from WAMP.** They're two separate things running side by side:

- **WAMP** serves the PHP API and web UI on port 80 locally
- **SSH tunnel** tells the remote server "forward incoming requests to my local port 80"

WAMP doesn't know or care about the tunnel. The tunnel doesn't know or care about WAMP. They just need to both be running at the same time.

---

**Not persistent by default** — you'd need to start both on boot. But you can automate both with Windows Task Scheduler so they start automatically when your computer starts, with no manual steps.

The startup sequence would be:

1. Task Scheduler starts WAMP on boot
2. Task Scheduler runs the SSH tunnel command on boot (with auto-reconnect if it drops)

WAMP already has a "start with Windows" option in its system tray menu. The SSH tunnel I'd wire up as a scheduled task with a restart-on-failure policy.

---

So the full picture once it's all set up:

- Power on your PC → WAMP starts, tunnel connects automatically
- Power off → tunnel drops, local API goes offline
- The remote Linux API stays live regardless (it's unaffected)
